episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Long-time listeners know Mengqiu as the popular guest from episode one. Our last conversation was in May of the previous year, when Beijing's pandemic lockdown was at its strictest, and this episode is a catch-up. We discuss this year's primary-market financing trends and today's rapidly accelerating ChatGPT, AI, and large-model wave. Mengqiu has authority on the topic: she is founding partner of Crystal Stream Capital and, before becoming an investor, was a technology VP at Baidu. We focus on whether large models will bring a new giant-company revolution and reset the landscape.

outline.textZh: 最近，我正忙着对自己的大脑进行预训练
outline.textEn: Recently I have been busy pretraining my own brain

outline.textZh: OpenAI的女性CTO Mira Murati 对C端产品发挥了关键作用，这带来iPhone时刻
outline.textEn: OpenAI CTO Mira Murati played a key role in the consumer product, bringing an iPhone moment

outline.textZh: 未来十年AI是长期有价值的方向，有solid价值基础
outline.textEn: AI will be a valuable long-term direction over the next decade, with a solid value foundation

outline.textZh: 每天一睁眼要是AI没新闻都隐约松口气……我从没有过这种感觉，even在我们的互联网1.0时代
outline.textEn: If I wake up and AI has no news, I faintly breathe a sigh of relief; I never felt this even in internet 1.0

outline.textZh: 为什么这次这么快？
outline.textEn: Why is this moving so fast?

outline.textZh: AI从1.0到2.0的范式变化
outline.textEn: The paradigm shift from AI 1.0 to AI 2.0

outline.textZh: DeepMind已经是世界上最奇怪的机构，但最后还是卖给了Google
outline.textEn: DeepMind was already the strangest organization in the world, but it still ultimately sold to Google

outline.textZh: 所有技术Google都有，但为什么Google没有做起来？
outline.textEn: Google has all the technology, so why did Google not make it work?

outline.textZh: 那么，OpenAI为什么可以做起来？（belief、钱、资源、人）
outline.textEn: Why was OpenAI able to make it work? Belief, money, resources, and people

outline.textZh: 2023当下，一级市场投资的水温：新能源、新材料、新消费、AI的投融资冷热
outline.textEn: The 2023 temperature of primary-market investing: financing heat and cold in new energy, new materials, new consumer, and AI

outline.textZh: 对AI项目，投资人看起来狂热，但出手很理智
outline.textEn: Investors look enthusiastic about AI projects but act very rationally

outline.textZh: 大模型时代的Killing App在哪里？什么时候会是iPhone 4时刻？
outline.textEn: Where is the killer app in the large-model era, and when will the iPhone 4 moment arrive?

alphaLens.alphaTitle: AI Heat, Few Fundable Targets
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Mengqiu separates long-term AI value from near-term venture investability. The market can be rational and excited at the same time because belief, money, resources, and people must all concentrate before a durable AI company appears.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: A hot AI narrative does not automatically create a broad early-stage opportunity set; scarcity of truly investable teams may be the dominant market fact.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Concentrate AI exposure in teams with rare combinations of technical talent, compute access, product taste, and capital endurance rather than spraying across headline-driven demos.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode is set in the fast-moving 2023 ChatGPT wave, while the primary market was still cold across new energy, new materials, consumer, and other sectors.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: AI is moving from AI 1.0 pattern-recognition systems to AI 2.0 general model platforms that can reset company boundaries over a decade.
evidenceSignals: Mengqiu calls AI a valuable long-term direction over the next decade
She says investors seem excited but remain rational
The notes state investable targets are fewer than headlines suggest
The episode discusses OpenAI's belief, money, resources, and people
It frames ChatGPT as an iPhone moment brought into a consumer product
watchlist: AI companies converting demos into recurring revenue
Compute financing and infrastructure partnerships
Founder teams with prior search, office, or platform experience
Primary-market funding breadth outside AI
Incumbent responses from Google, Microsoft, Baidu, and other platforms
risks: Notes-derived source does not provide company-specific diligence
AI platform economics may centralize around a few incumbents
Venture rounds can price in decade-long value too early
Fast model progress can obsolete application startups

thesisTable.evidence: The notes say investors are excited but rational and targets are few
thesisTable.implication: Due diligence should reject narrative-only AI startups

thesisTable.evidence: The source lists belief, money, resources, and people
thesisTable.implication: Single-advantage teams face structural disadvantages

thesisTable.evidence: Mengqiu frames AI as a decade-long direction
thesisTable.implication: Investors need endurance and milestone discipline

episodes.titleZh: 20. The Information前记者空降巴西，她的拉美商业游记
episodes.titleEn: 20. A Former The Information Reporter Lands in Brazil: Her Latin America Business Travel Notes
episodes.summaryEn: Yunan, a former reporter for The Information, shares observations from traveling in Brazil and Latin America as a technology journalist. The episode covers local delivery and mobility apps, Chinese companies in Brazil, business culture, venture funding, political instability, and advice for Chinese operators entering the Latin American market.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
这是一集很轻松的节目，聊天对象是我的好朋友Yunan，过去半年她在海外游历而且其中一站是遥远的南美——从巴西回国需要经历30多个小时的折腾。
Yunan是美国科技媒体The Information前记者，让我们看看当一名科技记者空降巴西，会发生点什么吧。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: This is a relaxed episode with my friend Yunan, who has been traveling overseas for the past half year. One stop was distant South America, and returning to China from Brazil required more than thirty hours of travel. Yunan is a former reporter at the U.S. technology media outlet The Information. In this conversation, we see what happens when a technology journalist lands in Brazil: from delivery and ride-hailing apps to tax policy, labor law, local venture capital, Chinese companies, middleman culture, Brazilian families, politics, inflation, and the feel of doing business in Latin America.

outline.textZh: 用葡萄牙语打招呼、介绍Yunan
outline.textEn: Greeting in Portuguese and introducing Yunan

outline.textZh: 一个科技记者为什么空降到拉美游历
outline.textEn: Why a technology journalist landed in Latin America to travel

outline.textEn: For Chinese people, where does Latin America rank as a business destination, and in which countries specifically?

outline.textEn: No local growth VC fund; SoftBank was once active but has now retreated

outline.textEn: Active U.S. dollar funds locally and exit methods

outline.textEn: Cultural contrast: a strong middleman culture, where cold email is not very useful

outline.textZh: 一个巴西人的典型周日，美国的小家庭vs巴西的大家庭文化
outline.textEn: A typical Brazilian Sunday, and American small-family culture vs. Brazilian extended-family culture

outline.textZh: 空降到圣保罗，怎么利用记者技能开拓本地圈子
outline.textEn: Landing in Sao Paulo and using reporting skills to open local circles

outline.textEn: Several types of Chinese companies in Brazil: traditional companies, hardware companies, and internet companies such as Kuaishou, Didi, and ByteDance

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Brazil Needs Operators, Not Tourists
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The Brazil opportunity described here is not a simple app-copying story; it is a market-entry and relationship-navigation problem. Tax policy, labor law, middleman culture, and missing growth capital turn local operating knowledge into the scarce asset.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: For Chinese companies, Latin America may be less attractive as a standalone consumer-app land grab than as a demand shock for localization, compliance, channel, and partnership infrastructure.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back Brazil-facing service layers that help Chinese operators handle tax, labor, hiring, local partners, payments, logistics, and government relationships.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode captures Chinese overseas ambition expanding beyond familiar Asia corridors while local growth VC appears thin and SoftBank's earlier regional intensity has retreated.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Globalization is shifting from exporting Chinese playbooks to embedding inside local business cultures with different trust and intermediary systems.
evidenceSignals: The episode covers Brazil's food delivery and mobility apps such as Rappi, iFood, 99, and Uber
It highlights tax policy, labor law, and a gap in mid-sized companies

Chinese companies in Brazil are explicitly discussed
watchlist: Chinese company formation and hiring activity in Sao Paulo
Brazil tax and labor compliance service demand
Local venture funding availability and exits
Partnership networks around mobility, delivery, and ecommerce
Currency, inflation, and political stability affecting expansion
risks: Notes-derived travel observations may not represent all of Latin America
Brazil's tax and labor complexity can erase operating margin
Political and inflation volatility can delay exits
Middleman dependence may create governance and corruption risk

thesisTable.claim: Local operating friction is the opportunity
thesisTable.evidence: The notes emphasize tax policy, labor law, and middleman culture
thesisTable.implication: Invest in services that reduce expansion friction

thesisTable.implication: Bootstrap or strategic-capital models may outperform VC-heavy ones

thesisTable.implication: Distribution moats may sit in local networks

episodes.titleZh: 19. 硅谷银行危机未结束：未来风险传导的3个可能
episodes.titleEn: 19. The Silicon Valley Bank Crisis Is Not Over: Three Possible Paths of Risk Transmission
episodes.summaryEn: Allen returns to analyze the Silicon Valley Bank crisis, including the technical causes, the Federal Reserve's rescue choice, SVB's special role in the startup ecosystem, and its ties to China. The episode argues that depositors may have been protected, but the crisis could still transmit through confidence, banking behavior, and the broader venture economy.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
上集节目发布后，听众留言说希望多多邀请Allen，没想到这么快又请他录了一期：震动全球的硅谷银行危机。
就在北京时间本周一，美联储宣布救市——保全了所有存储人的钱、却抛弃了股东利益。这集节目聊了危机的技术细节，包括成因、美联储的选择、svb（Silicon Valley Bank，硅谷银行）特殊性、与中国的纽带关系，同时也探讨了未来风险传导的3个可能性。对于把钱存在硅谷银行的创业公司和风投机构来说，他们的钱安全了。但是，这场危机尚未结束。
这会是一个更长线下坠的开端吗？用他的话来说，“这到底是end of the beginning还是beginning of the end？”
【嘉宾小记】
王怀南的职业生涯贯穿了上世纪90年代到现在。从清华半导体+英文系起步，到纽约的哥大社会学系求学，自此迈入美国的社会大熔炉。凭借着对社会的兴趣，毕业后在美国走了一条很少有华人走的路——做咨询、市场这些广泛与文化、人打交道的工作。他先后加入麦肯锡咨询、宝洁、Yahoo、Google，横跨美国最具有时代标志性的企业。直到2006年回国创业，是上市公司宝宝树、初创公司米茶科技创始人，也是陆奇创办的奇绩创坛合伙人、导师。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: After the previous episode was released, listeners said they hoped Allen would appear more often, and unexpectedly we invited him back so quickly to discuss the Silicon Valley Bank crisis that shook the world. On Monday Beijing time, the Federal Reserve announced a rescue: protecting all depositors' money while abandoning shareholder interests. This episode discusses the technical details of the crisis, including its causes, the Fed's choice, SVB's special nature, and its ties to China, while also exploring three possible paths of future risk transmission.

outline.textZh: 我们生在一个前所未有离奇的时代，聊最近震动世界的3起大事
outline.textEn: We live in an unprecedentedly strange era, and discuss three recent events that shook the world

outline.textZh: ChatGPT比OpenAI创始人自己Sam Altman的假设早了5-10年
outline.textEn: ChatGPT arrived five to ten years earlier than OpenAI founder Sam Altman's own assumptions

outline.textZh: 硅谷银行危机下，过去那个最紧张的周末、svb与中国的纽带关系
outline.textEn: The most tense weekend under the Silicon Valley Bank crisis, and SVB's ties to China

outline.textZh: 硅谷银行的标志性，但它管理危机的过程并不好
outline.textEn: Silicon Valley Bank was iconic, but it did not manage the crisis well

outline.textZh: 美联储做了个黑白分明的决定：保护了存款人、抛弃了股东，这背后的价值观
outline.textEn: The Fed made a black-and-white decision: protect depositors and abandon shareholders, and the values behind that

outline.textZh: 谈危机蔓延：令人担忧的未来风险传导的3个可能（内在信心的危机）
outline.textEn: Discussing crisis spread: three worrying possible risk-transmission paths and an internal confidence crisis

outline.textZh: ChatGPT远远超过工业革命对人类的影响、工业革命超过互联网对人类的影响
outline.textEn: ChatGPT's impact on humanity far exceeds the Industrial Revolution, and the Industrial Revolution exceeded the internet

outline.textZh: 硅谷银行危机成因、可能杜绝挤兑吗、错在何方
outline.textEn: Causes of the SVB crisis, whether a bank run could have been prevented, and where the mistakes were

outline.textZh: 硅谷银行的特殊意义，虽然破产了但仍然是一个好资产
outline.textEn: Silicon Valley Bank's special meaning: although bankrupt, it was still a good asset

outline.textZh: 从硅谷银行取出来的钱，存到哪里呢？
outline.textEn: Where should money withdrawn from Silicon Valley Bank go?

outline.textZh: 和2008年雷曼危机的对比：一开始就觉得这次不会是末日现象
outline.textEn: Comparing with the 2008 Lehman crisis: from the start, this did not seem like doomsday

outline.textZh: 世界的连接和魔幻：硅谷银行倒闭了，一群中国人很着急
outline.textEn: The connected and surreal world: Silicon Valley Bank collapsed, and a group of Chinese people became anxious

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Startup Banking Becomes Risk Infrastructure
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The SVB crisis showed that protecting deposits can stop one panic without restoring the old trust architecture around startup banking. The deeper alpha is that venture ecosystems had treated a specialized bank as infrastructure, not as a concentrated counterparty.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: The post-SVB opportunity is not only better banks; it is startup treasury software, diversified deposit rails, and risk controls that founders previously ignored.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back tools and financial services that make startup treasury diversification, counterparty monitoring, payroll continuity, and venture debt exposure visible by default.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode was recorded around the Fed's decision to protect depositors while abandoning shareholders, when founders were actively deciding where withdrawn SVB money should go.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Startup finance is moving from relationship-bank convenience to explicit resilience engineering across deposits, credit, payroll, and investor communications.
evidenceSignals: The source says the Fed protected depositors while abandoning shareholder interests
The episode focuses on three possible paths of risk transmission
SVB is described as iconic and specially meaningful to startups
The notes ask where money withdrawn from SVB should go
The guest argues SVB was bankrupt but still a good asset
watchlist: Founder adoption of multi-bank treasury policies
Startup payroll failures or near misses during bank stress
Venture debt availability after regional bank shocks
Bank concentration among VC-backed startups
Fintech products for insured cash sweep and counterparty monitoring
risks: Notes-derived source captures crisis analysis before all effects were visible
Founders may revert to convenience once panic fades
Treasury tools can become commoditized features inside banking platforms
Bank regulation can reduce but not eliminate confidence shocks

thesisTable.evidence: The notes discuss risk spread and an internal confidence crisis
thesisTable.implication: Products must manage behavior, not only balances

thesisTable.evidence: The episode calls out SVB's special meaning for startups
thesisTable.implication: Replacing SVB requires workflow integration, not generic banking

thesisTable.claim: Depositor rescue did not end the problem
thesisTable.evidence: The title says the crisis is not over despite Fed protection
thesisTable.implication: Risk tooling demand can persist after the acute event

episodes.titleZh: 18. 商业口述史：从美国往事，到Google输掉OpenAI第一局
episodes.titleEn: 18. Business Oral History: From American Stories to Google Losing the First Round to OpenAI
episodes.summaryEn: Wang Huainan, former Google Asia CMO and the person who named Google in Chinese, connects his career from McKinsey, P&G, Yahoo, and Google to the history behind today's ChatGPT moment. The episode uses his path through U.S. business and internet companies to discuss Yahoo's decline, Google's strengths and weaknesses, OpenAI, Sam Altman, and how major company structures shape AI competition.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
ChatGPT来了，Google拉响红色警报。本集邀请前Google亚洲区CMO，也是为Google中国取名“谷歌”的王怀南先生，来聊聊ChatGPT和紧急应战的谷歌。
这次聊天让我得到了很多意料之外的惊喜。王怀南作为上世纪90年代赴美留学、在外企的第一批华人高管，他的经历刚好钩沉出一段美国往事。今天包括OpenAI创始人Sam Altman、奇绩创坛创始人兼CEO陆奇、微软CEO Satya Nadella、LinkedIn CEO Jeff Weiner等等这些处在世界舞台中心的大人物全部交错在这段历史之中。
正改写人类命运的ChatGPT，也正是从这段往事中演化出来。
【嘉宾小记】
他的职业生涯贯穿了上世纪90年代到现在。从清华半导体+英文系起步，到纽约的哥大社会学系求学，自此迈入美国的社会大熔炉。凭借着对社会的兴趣，毕业后在美国走了一条很少有华人走的路——做咨询、市场这些广泛与文化、人打交道的工作。他先后加入麦肯锡咨询、宝洁、Yahoo、Google，横跨美国最具有时代标志性的企业。直到2006年回国创业，是上市公司宝宝树、初创公司米茶科技创始人，也是陆奇创办的奇绩创坛合伙人、导师。
01:52 一个华人的历史钩沉出一段美国往事

•  24:58 雅虎败了，陆奇去了微软，我去了Google

•  32:55 同样是收购，为什么Google多成功、雅虎多
episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: ChatGPT has arrived, and Google has sounded a red alert. This episode invites Wang Huainan, former Google Asia CMO and the person who gave Google its Chinese name, to discuss ChatGPT and Google's emergency response. The conversation brought many unexpected surprises. Wang's experience as one of the first Chinese executives in foreign companies after studying in the United States in the 1990s uncovers a chapter of American history. Figures such as OpenAI founder Sam Altman, MiraclePlus founder and CEO Lu Qi, Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella, and LinkedIn CEO Jeff Weiner all intersect with this history.

outline.textZh: 一个华人的历史钩沉出一段美国往事
outline.textEn: A Chinese person's history uncovers a chapter of American history

outline.textZh: 在上世纪90年代，背靠半个世纪产物的麦肯锡
outline.textEn: McKinsey in the 1990s, backed by half a century of accumulated work

outline.textZh: 从风云演绎的理论家，到美国宝洁艰难生存
outline.textEn: From a theorist of grand change to struggling to survive at P&G in America

outline.textZh: 消费品带着强悍的文化色彩，作为外来者重新定义了品客薯片
outline.textEn: Consumer goods carry a strong cultural color; redefining Pringles as an outsider

outline.textZh: 2001年，媒体巨变，一句名言是“money follows eyeball”
outline.textEn: In 2001, media changed dramatically, and the saying was "money follows eyeball"

outline.textZh: 奋然一跳到雅虎，每天想辞职——这里尊重工程师、尊重销售，但不尊重做品牌的人 “我在雅虎认识的第一个人是陆奇”
outline.textEn: Taking the leap to Yahoo and wanting to quit every day: it respected engineers and salespeople, but not brand people. "The first person I met at Yahoo was Lu Qi"

outline.textZh: 我和陆奇在搜索和平台事业部，汇报给一个人：Jeff Weiner（现在是LinkedIn CEO）
outline.textEn: Lu Qi and I were in the search and platform division, reporting to the same person: Jeff Weiner, now LinkedIn CEO

outline.textZh: 在雅虎，我们全面开战
outline.textEn: At Yahoo, we fought on all fronts

outline.textZh: 彼时的战友去哪了：一个人创立Snapchat、一个人当上LinkedIn CEO，一个人投资Facebook，一个人代表雅虎创立YC——美国互联网很多事情从这里展开
outline.textEn: Where those comrades went: one founded Snapchat, one became LinkedIn CEO, one invested in Facebook, and one represented Yahoo in founding YC; much of the U.S. internet unfolded from here

outline.textZh: 雅虎败了，陆奇去了微软，我去了Google
outline.textEn: Yahoo lost, Lu Qi went to Microsoft, and I went to Google

outline.textZh: （陆奇后来是微软二号位、必应创始人，差点成了微软CEO）.雅虎和谷歌的两种命运
outline.textEn: Lu Qi later became Microsoft's number two, founded Bing, and nearly became Microsoft CEO; the two destinies of Yahoo and Google

outline.textZh: 雅虎是没有灵魂的雅虎
outline.textEn: Yahoo was a Yahoo without a soul

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Organization Beat Google's AI Inventory
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Wang Huainan's oral history turns Google's ChatGPT shock into an organizational case study rather than a pure technology race. The hidden insight is that the people, incentives, and product posture around a capability can matter more than possessing the capability first.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Google's first-round loss to OpenAI was not mainly about lacking AI assets; it was about structure, urgency, and the ability to turn research into a destabilizing consumer product.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Invest in AI companies and partnerships that combine research capability with permission to ship disruptive products, especially where incumbents cannot self-cannibalize cleanly.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode is anchored in Google's red alert after ChatGPT, using earlier Yahoo and Google history to explain why large companies can miss platform transitions.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: AI competition is shifting from owning research labs to designing organizations that can convert research into fast-moving products and ecosystems.
evidenceSignals: The source says Google sounded a red alert after ChatGPT
The discussion connects Yahoo's decline with Google's strengths and weaknesses
The episode includes OpenAI, Sam Altman, Microsoft, Satya Nadella, and Lu Qi
Yahoo is described as fighting on all fronts and losing
The guest's career links McKinsey, P&G, Yahoo, and Google as organizational histories
watchlist: Incumbent AI product launches that cannibalize existing revenue
Partnerships between research groups and distribution platforms
Talent flows from large labs to independent startups
Consumer adoption of AI products outside search incumbents
Internal reorganizations at major AI incumbents
risks: Notes-derived source gives a historical frame, not a full competitive dataset
Incumbents can recover through distribution and capital
Startups may depend on incumbents for cloud, compute, or channels
Consumer AI usage can fragment before monetization matures

thesisTable.evidence: The episode asks why Google did not make ChatGPT work first
thesisTable.implication: AI diligence must include organizational constraints

thesisTable.evidence: Yahoo's decline is used as a bridge to Google's AI problem
thesisTable.implication: Incumbent risk should be evaluated through prior platform transitions

thesisTable.evidence: The notes trace figures from Yahoo to LinkedIn, Microsoft, YC, and Google
thesisTable.implication: Talent networks can predict where new platform power accumulates

episodes.titleZh: 17.【趋势2023】沉闷的港股会好吗、富豪的家办与苦恼
episodes.titleEn: 17. [Trends 2023] Will Dull Hong Kong Stocks Improve, and What Troubles Do the Wealthy Face With Family Offices?
episodes.summaryEn: Tina discusses 2023 Hong Kong stock trends, Hong Kong IPOs, and the rise of family offices across Hong Kong, Singapore, and Dubai. The episode uses family offices and trusts to explore wealth management, inheritance, values, hidden clauses, family conflict, and the very specific worries of high-net-worth families.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
富豪有富豪的苦恼。
富人的部分痛苦来源于“钱多了、情就薄了”。最近香港名媛蔡天凤碎尸案就是极端案例。
今天这集节目，我和田娜女士不仅聊了2023港股走势、赴港IPO趋势，也借着聊目前在富人群体中盛行的家族办公室，探讨了许多有关金钱、人性与价值观的话题。原来，很多富豪对遗产传承是有价值观要求的，而且他们将这些条条框框、那些你完全想象不到的细节，写进了家族信托的隐秘条款里——人走了，条款一直在。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: The wealthy have their own troubles. Part of rich people's pain comes from the fact that when money becomes abundant, affection can become thin. The recent Hong Kong socialite Abby Choi dismemberment case is an extreme example. In this episode, Tina and I discuss 2023 Hong Kong stock trends and Hong Kong IPO prospects, while also using family offices, now popular among the wealthy, to explore money, human nature, and values. Many wealthy people have value requirements for inheritance and write incredibly detailed clauses into family trusts.

outline.textEn: Hong Kong capital development has passed through four eras

outline.textEn: The cold, heat, and forecast for Hong Kong IPOs: when Ant Group was going public, global institutional money came; when it did not, where did that money go?

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Hong Kong's Fifth Era Is Wealth Infrastructure
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The family-office discussion reframes dull Hong Kong stocks as a transition from listing venue to wealth-governance hub. The investable layer is not whether one IPO pops, but whether Hong Kong captures inheritance, trust, and cross-border allocation workflows from wealthy families.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Hong Kong's recovery path may be stronger in private wealth infrastructure than in a broad IPO or equity-market rebound.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back services around family offices, trusts, cross-border tax planning, succession governance, and private-market allocation rather than relying on generic Hong Kong stock beta.
alphaLens.whyNow: The source says Hong Kong, Singapore, and Dubai are all trying to attract family offices while Hong Kong's IPO market and stock confidence remain uncertain.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Hong Kong capital markets are entering a fifth era where wealth management and family-governance services become strategic infrastructure.
evidenceSignals: The episode explicitly says Hong Kong capital development is entering a fifth era: wealth management
It compares Hong Kong, Singapore, and Dubai efforts to attract family offices
The notes discuss uncertainty, confidence, liquidity, and systematic buying
The IPO section asks where global institutional money went after Ant did not list
Family trusts with detailed value clauses are discussed
watchlist: Hong Kong family-office formation and tax-concession uptake
Flows between Hong Kong, Singapore, and Dubai wealth platforms
Hong Kong IPO pipeline quality and first-day performance
Trust disputes and demand for governance advisory
Liquidity and confidence indicators in Hong Kong equities
risks: Notes-derived source mixes market outlook with social observations
Wealth-management growth may not translate into public equity returns
Hong Kong faces competition from Singapore and Dubai
Family-office assets can be opaque and slow to monetize

thesisTable.claim: The fifth era is wealth management
thesisTable.evidence: The notes use that exact framing for Hong Kong capital development
thesisTable.implication: Look beyond IPO volume for Hong Kong financial-services alpha

thesisTable.claim: Family conflict creates demand for structure
thesisTable.evidence: The source discusses detailed family-trust clauses and wealthy-family worries
thesisTable.implication: Governance and trust services can be durable revenue pools

thesisTable.evidence: The episode asks whether dull Hong Kong stocks and IPOs will improve
thesisTable.implication: Public-market timing should not be the only thesis driver

episodes.titleZh: 16.【趋势2023】那些挣到钱、消失的互联网中高层去哪了
episodes.titleEn: 16. [Trends 2023] Where Did the Internet Middle and Senior Managers Who Made Money and Disappeared Go?
episodes.summaryEn: Colin discusses the collective departure of mid- and senior-level internet managers, their choices after leaving, and the roots of the shift. The episode also reviews the four best entry points during China's previous internet golden age and asks whether today could be another good moment to board for the next twenty years.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
这集播客中，我们不仅探讨了互联网中高层“集体出走”的比例、类型和根源，讨论了顶级职业经理人的可能回报，同时也复盘了上一个黄金二十年中的四个最佳“上车”时点。在Colin看来，今天也许是面向未来二十年的另一个不错的“上车”时机。
这档节目正在推出【趋势2023】系列特辑，嘉宾包括创业者、一级市场和二级市场投资人。如果你想了解2023年最新的商业趋势、科技前沿和时代面貌，欢迎订阅《商业访谈录》。
【嘉宾小记】
Colin的职业生涯很长很长，从大学生创业计划“挑战杯”金奖到宝洁到波士顿咨询到百度副总裁再到美的总裁办总监，现在是爱博医疗的持股公司爱博星辰CEO。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: In this podcast episode, we discuss not only the proportion, types, and roots of the collective departure of mid- and senior-level internet managers, but also the possible rewards for top professional managers. We also review the four best moments to get on board during the previous golden twenty years. In Colin's view, today may be another good boarding opportunity for the next twenty years. Colin's long career runs from winning the Challenge Cup entrepreneurship gold award to P&G, BCG, vice president at Baidu, director in Midea's president office, and now CEO of Aibo Star.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Ex-Internet Managers Become the New Operating System
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The disappearance of mid- and senior-level internet managers is a talent reallocation event, not just a career story. The Shein example suggests the next alpha may come from applying internet operating methods to traditional industries, supply chains, and global commerce.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: The decline of platform-internet prestige may increase returns for companies that absorb ex-internet operators into less digitized sectors.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back businesses that combine internet growth, data, and product management with manufacturing, consumer supply chains, exports, and traditional enterprise operations.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode frames 2023 as a possible new boarding point after a collective departure of internet managers and a crisis in next-generation management talent.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: China's operator class is moving from platform companies toward hybrid businesses where digital methods meet physical production and global distribution.

episodes.titleZh: 15. 到美利坚去创业吧
episodes.titleEn: 15. Go to America to Start a Company
episodes.summaryEn: Three Chinese guests who understand both Chinese and U.S. culture discuss why a new generation of entrepreneurs wants to enter the American market early and build local companies there. The episode frames this as Globalization 3.0 and covers go-to-market, storytelling, cultural friction, community, hiring, and what Chinese founders need most in the United States.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
逆全球化时代下，企业家正做出截然相反的选择：他们更早更快崭露出强劲的出海野心，第一站就希望占领高地：美国。这对过去的中国创业者来说，是不可想象的。
在新时代路口，我邀请了三位嘉宾。她们都是华人，在人生不同阶段赴美，熟悉双边文化面貌；现在她们致力于帮海外企业进入美国市场。你会发现，在我们重新与世界连结的2023年伊始，世界变了，全球化也变了。
Part 1：全球化3.0
•  1:18 三人自我介绍
•  5:10 聊天的起点是Sophia的两个观点：1、going global is going local；2、不是跨境创业，而是如何到美国去做一家本土企业
•  7:50 Joyce去过100多个国家，她的观点是：think global, act local
•  10:12 为什么如今全球化被提到了全新高度？globalization 3.0（全球化3.0） VS deglobalization（去全球化）
•  15:22 全球化新特征：equalization（均衡化）
•  16:20 为什么现在是出海的好时代？
•  17:15 不是巨头才能出海，创业公司从第一天就可以建立全球框架、思考美国市场
•  20:55 创始人要有growth mindset
•  23:35 过去三年的全球化趋势观察：本地人心态的转变、Zoom使人们更均衡Part 2：中国创业者赴美

•  75:30 对比：在美国的犹太人、中国人、印度人、欧洲人
•  84:12 美国不只是资本主义国家，也是人和关系的社会
•  88:30 华人群体缺乏互帮互助的文化（sense of c
episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: In the age of deglobalization, entrepreneurs are making sharply opposite choices: they are showing stronger overseas ambitions earlier and faster, and they want their first stop to be the high ground of the United States. For Chinese entrepreneurs in the past, this was unimaginable. At this new crossroads, I invited three guests. They are all Chinese, went to the United States at different life stages, understand both cultures, and now help overseas companies enter the U.S. market. At the start of 2023, as we reconnect with the world, the world has changed and globalization has changed.

outline.textZh: 三人自我介绍
outline.textEn: The three guests introduce themselves

outline.textZh: 聊天的起点是Sophia的两个观点：1、going global is going local；2、不是跨境创业，而是如何到美国去做一家本土企业
outline.textEn: The starting point is Sophia's two views: going global is going local, and this is not cross-border entrepreneurship but building a local company in America

outline.textZh: Joyce去过100多个国家，她的观点是：think global, act local
outline.textEn: Joyce has visited more than one hundred countries, and her view is: think global, act local

outline.textEn: Why globalization is now raised to a new height: globalization 3.0 vs. deglobalization

outline.textZh: 全球化新特征：equalization（均衡化）
outline.textEn: A new feature of globalization: equalization

outline.textZh: 为什么现在是出海的好时代？
outline.textEn: Why now is a good era for going global

outline.textZh: 不是巨头才能出海，创业公司从第一天就可以建立全球框架、思考美国市场
outline.textEn: Going global is not only for giants; startups can build a global framework and think about the U.S. market from day one

outline.textZh: 创始人要有growth mindset
outline.textEn: Founders need a growth mindset

outline.textZh: 过去三年的全球化趋势观察：本地人心态的转变、Zoom使人们更均衡Part 2：中国创业者赴美
outline.textEn: Globalization trends over the past three years: changing local mindsets, Zoom making people more equal, and the start of Part 2 on Chinese founders going to America

outline.textZh: 中国公司常遇到的问题
outline.textEn: Common problems Chinese companies encounter

outline.textEn: Many details of cultural difference, including age, photos, and questions about childbirth

outline.textZh: go to market的难题
outline.textEn: The go-to-market challenge

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Going Global Means Becoming Local First
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The episode's strongest claim is that Chinese founders are no longer treating the United States as a late expansion market. Globalization 3.0 means building a local American company from day one, with storytelling, hiring, and community as core product work.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: The US opportunity for Chinese entrepreneurs is not cross-border arbitrage; it is local company formation by founders who can operate biculturally.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Invest in Chinese-origin startups that design US go-to-market, brand narrative, hiring, legal setup, and community trust into the company at inception.
alphaLens.whyNow: The source says deglobalization has produced the opposite founder behavior: stronger overseas ambition earlier and faster, with the US seen as a high ground.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Chinese startup globalization is moving from export-led expansion to equalized, locally embedded company building.
evidenceSignals: Sophia says going global is going local
Joyce says think global and act local
The episode distinguishes globalization 3.0 from deglobalization
The notes say startups can think about the US market from day one
Common problems Chinese companies encounter in America are explicitly discussed
watchlist: Chinese founders incorporating and hiring first teams in the US
US-focused pitch narratives from China-origin startups
Local advisors, community builders, and GTM agencies
Customer trust barriers for Chinese brands
Visa, compliance, and data-localization requirements
risks: Notes-derived source is advice-heavy and may understate execution difficulty
US-China political tension can impair trust and fundraising
Founders may confuse localization with translation
Hiring local executives without power can create false localization

thesisTable.implication: Fund companies that localize decision-making, not only sales copy

thesisTable.evidence: The source says startups can build a global framework from day one
thesisTable.implication: US-readiness can be an early diligence criterion

thesisTable.evidence: The episode frames stronger overseas ambition in an age of deglobalization
thesisTable.implication: Founders must build trust before geopolitical friction hardens

episodes.titleZh: 14. 香港的语言政策、暧昧和青年迷思
episodes.titleEn: 14. Hong Kong's Language Policy, Ambiguity, and Youth Confusion
episodes.summaryEn: Returning guest Yuxiang uses Hong Kong's language policy as an entry point to discuss the city's identity, economy, and youth anxieties. By comparing Hong Kong with Singapore, the conversation explores bilingual policy, dialect suppression, educational tradeoffs, the loss and luck of linguistic complexity, and what Hong Kong can do next.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
宇翔跟我们详细聊过东南亚11国，但作为香港人的他，对本土更有发言权。这集就和老朋友以语言政策为切口聊聊香港。
香港今天在语言政策上展现的迷惘，正是这座城市真实的精神状态。
•  00:53 香港的"两文三语"政策需要调整吗？论其混杂性
•  13:20 "如果中文和英文水平相当，我大概率是一个头脑不太灵光的人"

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Yuxiang previously spoke with us in detail about eleven Southeast Asian countries, but as a Hong Konger, he has more authority on his own city. In this episode, we talk with an old friend about Hong Kong through the lens of language policy. The confusion Hong Kong shows today in language policy is the city's real spiritual state. The discussion asks whether Hong Kong's policy of two written languages and three spoken languages needs adjustment, compares Singapore's shift from bilingual balance to English as the main teaching language and the elimination of dialects, and turns to Hong Kong youth.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Language Policy Is Economic Strategy
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Hong Kong's language ambiguity is presented as a symptom of its deeper economic and identity uncertainty. The investable insight is that bilingual complexity can be either a tax on youth or a bridge capability if institutions turn it into professional advantage.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Hong Kong should not be evaluated only by finance policy; human-capital design around language may determine whether it remains a regional connector.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back education, credentialing, recruiting, and workplace tools that convert Cantonese, Mandarin, and English complexity into cross-border professional mobility.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode links language-policy confusion with Hong Kong's economic repositioning and youth anxieties, while comparing Singapore's more forceful language choices.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Hong Kong's inherited linguistic hybridity is moving from automatic advantage to a management problem requiring deliberate education and labor-market design.
evidenceSignals: The episode asks whether two written languages and three spoken languages need adjustment
It compares Hong Kong with Singapore's shift toward English as the main teaching language
The notes discuss dialect suppression and the costs of Singapore's policy
Hong Kong youth confusion is a central topic
The source connects language policy with Hong Kong's economic repositioning
watchlist: Language-of-instruction policy changes in Hong Kong schools
Youth employment outcomes by language capability
Cross-border roles requiring Cantonese, Mandarin, and English
Education products for professional bilingualism
Migration flows between Hong Kong, mainland China, and Singapore
risks: Notes-derived source is sociopolitical rather than company-specific
Education policy changes can take years to affect labor markets
Language issues are politically sensitive and hard to monetize directly
Hong Kong's ambiguity may remain unresolved

thesisTable.evidence: The source says language policy reflects Hong Kong's spiritual state
thesisTable.implication: Human-capital strategy is central to Hong Kong's competitiveness

thesisTable.evidence: The episode contrasts Hong Kong with Singapore's English-led policy
thesisTable.implication: Policy clarity can trade cultural richness for economic focus

thesisTable.claim: Complexity can become a bridge asset
thesisTable.evidence: The notes describe Hong Kong's luck and misfortune in linguistic complexity
thesisTable.implication: Tools that manage multilingual work can capture value

episodes.titleZh: 13. 邮轮小史，去寻找海面上的乌托邦
episodes.titleEn: 13. A Short History of Cruises: Searching for Utopia on the Sea
episodes.summaryEn: Cruise-industry veteran Jianbin, who has boarded more than one hundred ships, describes cruises as utopias at sea and traces the industry's history, major players, ship categories, pricing, and passenger experience. The conversation moves from luxury vessels and Antarctic journeys to the Chinese cruise market and why cruises can offer a distinctive spiritual encounter with the world.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
建斌在过去十几年登上过100多艘邮轮，他说邮轮像“海上的乌托邦”。在一艘艘远离陆地和现实的乌托邦上，他目睹过毕加索真迹，看见过火山在黑夜里喷发、红色的岩浆流入海里，而前不久他刚从南极归来——邮轮带他走到世界尽头，去和世界有了一场对话。
希望在2023年邮轮与大海能慰藉你、带给你与众不同的精神体验。
•  03:16 自我介绍，从传统旅游闯入邮轮行业
•  06:24 一部邮轮小史
•  13:55 5家邮轮巨头的爱恨情仇——嘉年华、皇家加勒比、诺唯真、MSC地中海、云顶（疫情中破产）
•  22:46“教母”
•  26:13 很特别的邮轮：玛丽皇后二号（远洋邮轮、遵循贵族传统可以带宠物、old money）

•  32:15 关键指标：人均服务比、人均空间比

•  37:40 漂泊在“海上的乌托邦”之上，看着日出、日落、星空、大海、邮轮靠港和远去
•  39:02 过去两个月：游历（美国、智利、南极、中国香港）、忐忑和疫情后的世界

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Jianbin has boarded more than one hundred cruise ships over the past decade-plus, and he says cruises are like utopias at sea. On ships far from land and reality, he has seen original Picassos, witnessed a volcano erupting at night with red lava flowing into the sea, and recently returned from Antarctica. Cruises have taken him to the end of the world and into a dialogue with it. We hope that in 2023, cruises and the sea can comfort you and bring a distinctive spiritual experience.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Cruises Sell Managed Utopia
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Jianbin's account suggests cruises are not merely travel inventory but engineered worlds with measurable luxury physics. Staff-to-guest ratio, space-per-guest ratio, route rarity, and curated cultural encounters create pricing power beyond standard tourism recovery.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: The premium cruise opportunity is less about mass-market capacity and more about high-end experience design where ships become scarce, self-contained destinations.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Look for cruise, expedition, and luxury-travel businesses with differentiated routes, high service density, and China-facing demand recovery rather than undifferentiated berth volume.
alphaLens.whyNow: The source frames 2023 as a moment when cruises and the sea could reenter travelers' lives after pandemic disruption, while explaining the industry's major players and categories.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Cruising is moving from transport-plus-hotel packaging toward experiential infrastructure for affluent travelers seeking remoteness, safety, and symbolic escape.

episodes.titleZh: 12. 和黄宇翔聊东南亚另5国：小国的扈从和对冲
episodes.titleEn: 12. Talking With Huang Yuxiang About Five More Southeast Asian Countries: Small-State Bandwagoning and Hedging
episodes.summaryEn: This follow-up to the earlier Southeast Asia episode turns to Myanmar, Laos, Cambodia, Brunei, and East Timor. The discussion uses the ideas of bandwagoning and hedging to ask how small states manage great-power pressure, while also noting how far Chinese entrepreneurs have begun to reach in the region.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
之前和宇翔聊过《东南亚6国：政治、杀戮和国父》 (https://www.xiaoyuzhoufm.com/episodes/636fb238a5004aa3aa57144b) ，那一期反馈不错，听众提出也想听其他几个东南亚国家。我们讨论推出下集：东南亚另5国。这次我们将把视线放在5个小国上，由近及远是：缅甸——老挝——柬埔寨——文莱——东帝汶。
商业有个词是“对冲”，指特意减低另一项投资风险的投资，让我们看看这些小国家是怎样在大国地缘政治博弈中实现“政治对冲”的。
另一点，令我惊奇的是，中国人出去创业做生意范围如此广，已经有人把触角伸向了东南亚最偏远角落、也是世界最不发达的国家之一：东帝汶。

•  经济援助依靠中国、越南、泰国
•  中国、日本在东南亚投资最多，美国搞事情多33:36 第九站：柬埔寨
•  曾经辉煌（高棉帝国、吴哥文明），因何没落？
•  “自我屠杀”
•  君主基本没有权力
•  经济潜力处东南亚二梯队45:39 第十站：文莱

•  石油、天然气赚的钱用去建福利体系，经济多元化刚开始

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: After the previous conversation with Yuxiang about six Southeast Asian countries received good feedback, listeners asked for the other countries too. This episode turns to five smaller countries, moving outward from Myanmar to Laos, Cambodia, Brunei, and East Timor. In business, hedging means an investment made to reduce another risk; here we look at how small countries carry out political hedging amid great-power geopolitics. Another surprise is how widely Chinese people now go abroad to start businesses, even reaching East Timor, one of Southeast Asia's most remote corners and one of the world's least developed countries.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Small-State Hedging Is Market Structure
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The episode treats Myanmar, Laos, Cambodia, Brunei, and East Timor not as peripheral markets but as examples of how small states manage pressure. For Chinese entrepreneurs, bandwagoning and hedging are not political background noise; they shape licenses, partners, corridors, and risk limits.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Southeast Asia should be diligenced as a portfolio of political strategies, not as a single growth region.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back regional operating intelligence, compliance, logistics, and local-partner networks that help founders navigate country-specific hedging behavior.
alphaLens.whyNow: The source says Chinese entrepreneurs are reaching even East Timor, while great-power pressure makes small-state alignment choices more commercially relevant.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Business expansion in Southeast Asia is moving from simple market entry to geopolitical navigation across small states with different survival strategies.
evidenceSignals: The episode explicitly uses bandwagoning and hedging to analyze small states
It covers Myanmar, Laos, Cambodia, Brunei, and East Timor
East Timor is described as remote and among the world's least developed countries
The notes say Chinese entrepreneurs have begun reaching widely across the region
Political hedging is compared to risk reduction in investing
watchlist: Chinese founder activity in Myanmar, Laos, Cambodia, Brunei, and East Timor
Infrastructure and logistics corridors tied to great-power influence
License or concession changes after political shifts
Local partner quality and ownership rules
Security risk in Myanmar and frontier regions
risks: Notes-derived source is geopolitical and not company-specific
Small markets may lack scale even when entry is possible
Political instability can strand assets
Local alignment can become a liability if great-power pressure changes

episodes.titleZh: 11. 新加坡的淘金热和政商环境
episodes.titleEn: 11. Singapore's Gold Rush and Its Political-Business Environment
episodes.summaryEn: Neil Han, who has lived in Singapore for 16 years, recounts the city-state's waves of Web2 and Web3 business activity and its shift from the edge toward a regional hub. The episode digs into government grants, Temasek, official management, migration pressure, rent spikes, and why Singapore may be a hub rather than the center of Web3.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
Neil HAN（轶东）北大毕业后前往新加坡，在狮城16年。他在新加坡当过全球跨国公司高管、自己多次创业，亲身经历了新加坡从Web 2到Web 3的商业小浪、中浪和大浪——新加坡也在淘金热潮中，从边缘走向了枢纽。
这集我们聊了聊新加坡淘金热和政商环境。他聊到许多只有本地人才了解的细节——比如新加坡主权基金淡马锡，政府对官员的管理机制，企业怎么从政府拿钱等等。他们甚至调侃某家政府机构为“慈善组织”。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Neil Han went to Singapore after graduating from Peking University and has spent 16 years there. He has worked as a senior executive at a global multinational and started companies several times, experiencing Singapore's small, medium, and large business waves from Web2 to Web3. This episode discusses Singapore's gold rush and political-business environment, including details only locals tend to know: Temasek, how the government manages officials, how companies obtain government money, and why some entrepreneurs jokingly call one government-linked agency a charity.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Singapore Is the Hub, Not the Center
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Neil Han's Singapore account separates hub value from native-market dominance. Government grants, Temasek, rent pressure, migration, and Web3 inflows make Singapore powerful as a coordination point, but not necessarily the place where all end-user value is created.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: The stronger Singapore trade may be infrastructure for regional headquarters, treasury, compliance, and talent mobility rather than speculative Web3 startups themselves.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back Singapore-based services that help companies manage regional incorporation, grants, regulation, treasury, crypto compliance, and cross-border talent.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode describes Web2 and Web3 business waves, migration pressure, rent spikes, and the arrival of Web3 believers in Singapore's political-business environment.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Singapore is shifting from regional edge city to institutional gateway for Asia capital, founders, and regulated digital assets.

episodes.titleZh: 10. 加州土著谈硅谷精神与职场：You can be totally weird!
episodes.titleEn: 10. A California Native on the Silicon Valley Spirit and Workplace: You Can Be Totally Weird!
episodes.summaryEn: Sophia Xiao, a California native who has lived there for 35 years, describes Silicon Valley from a local perspective. The conversation covers immigrant culture, job-hopping careers, fundraising challenges for Chinese founders in the United States, storytelling in pitch decks, and how Americans view companies such as TikTok, SHEIN, Wish, and Temu.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
硅谷是移民的聚集地，土著是少数人。这期我邀请的Sophia Xiao（萧慧平）就是加州土著，从小在这生活迄今35年。她将从本地人的视角轻松聊聊，硅谷职场文化、中国创业者到美国融资会遇到的困境，以及美国人如何看赴美做生意的中国企业。
Sophia的职场选择体现了硅谷高速流转的职场文化。我联想到谢丽尔·桑德伯格在《向前一步》分享的一个观点：
人们常用“梯子”比喻职业生涯，但这个概念已不再适用大多数人。据统计，2010年至今，美国人在18-44岁之间会换11次工作。这意味着加入一个组织或进入一个公司，待在那里一步步往上爬的时代已经过去了。“职业生涯是方格架，不是竖梯。”
竖梯会限制人的行动，要么往上爬要么往下退，要么站在阶梯上要么跌下来；而方格架能让一个人拥有更多探索的可能。要爬到梯子顶端只有一种方式，但要爬到方格架的顶端则有很多种方式。方格架的比喻适用每个人，尤其适合处于职业初期、转行阶段、由于外部障碍止步不前，或休息一段时间准备重新进入职场的女性。和僵直的梯子相比，女性若能够走出一条时上时下、迂回曲折甚至偶尔误入死胡同的独特路线，才会为自我实现提供更好的机会。站在竖梯上，大多数攀爬者都不得不盯着上面一个人的屁股。
02:05 个人经历：
•  上世纪80年代从上海移民旧金山
•  1000美金重建生活
•  15岁打工：遇到抢银行、对方用枪指着我说：“我不想伤害任何人，我只要钱。”
•  FBI也来了，抢银行破案几率很小
•  美式VS亚洲教育：美国对青少年保护没那么好
•  15-18岁的社会教育：做零售业，小孩应该看老板脸色20:18 硅谷职场：
•  乐观土地
•  职场经历：IBM（闪辞）、BOX（上市）、Twitch（亚马逊收购）、美国一线投资公司
•  两年是节点
•  参加一个火箭
•  It’s never about your next role, it’s about your next-next role
•  创造工作而不是填补空缺
•  做ambiguous（模棱两可）的工作
•  中国创业者去美国融资因何受挫？
•  What’s missing is sometimes the storytelling
•  pitch deck（融资演讲稿）只需要10个slides
•  在上两个萧条中受影响的人，现在怎么样了？
•  美国人怎么看中国公司：TikTok、SHEIN、Wish（争议性）
•  美版拼多多Temu虽然便宜，但在culture上做得很好
•  Going global is going local
•  细数亚洲人在美国
episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Silicon Valley is a gathering place for immigrants, and natives are a minority. Sophia Xiao is one of those California natives, having grown up and lived there for 35 years. From a local perspective, she talks casually about Silicon Valley workplace culture, the difficulties Chinese entrepreneurs encounter when raising money in the United States, and how Americans view Chinese companies doing business there. Her own career choices reflect Silicon Valley's fast-moving labor culture, where a career is more like a lattice than a vertical ladder.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Silicon Valley Trust Is a Storytelling Market
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Sophia Xiao's local perspective implies Chinese founders often face a narrative and trust deficit before they face a product deficit. Silicon Valley's career lattice and tolerance for weirdness reward founders who can be legible locally without becoming generic.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: For Chinese startups in the US, pitch storytelling and cultural fluency can be as investable as product localization.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back founder-enablement services and startups that solve US fundraising narrative, local executive hiring, community trust, and brand perception for China-origin companies.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode names TikTok, SHEIN, Wish, and Temu as companies shaping how Americans view Chinese businesses, while Chinese founders are trying to raise and build in the US.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Cross-border entrepreneurship is moving from technical execution advantage to local trust, narrative, and talent-network advantage.

The notes describe Silicon Valley careers as lattices rather than ladders

watchlist: Fundraising conversion rates for China-origin US startups

episodes.titleEn: 9. Bonus English Original: An Oral History of Silicon Valley Venture Capital
episodes.summaryEn: This bonus episode lets listeners hear Sebastian Mallaby in the original English after the prior translated interview. Mallaby explains why The Power Law is a key to venture capital, recounts the personalities and institutions behind Silicon Valley VC, and compares its networks and downturn responses with China's venture scene.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
上期很多听众留言说希望听作家原声，所以临时加更一期。
塞巴斯蒂安·马拉比（Sebastian Mallaby）是一位绅士的英国记者和作家，出版过记录对冲基金历史的《More Money Than God》（富可敌国）、格林斯潘传记《The Man Who Knew》（格林斯潘传），两次入围普利策。他的妻子是知名期刊《经济学人》总编辑。
马拉比的新书《The Power Law》（风险投资史）揭露了硅谷风投的内幕故事。这期节目里，你将听到一部由他口述的硅谷风投史。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: After the previous episode, many listeners said they wanted to hear the writer's original voice, so this bonus episode was added. Sebastian Mallaby is a British journalist and author whose books include More Money Than God, a history of hedge funds, and The Man Who Knew, a biography of Alan Greenspan; he has twice been a Pulitzer finalist. His new book The Power Law reveals inside stories from Silicon Valley venture capital. In this episode, listeners hear a Silicon Valley VC history told orally by Mallaby himself.

episodes.transcriptEnExcerpt: [Music] hello for the power [Music] fore the power I chose the power law because it's sort of the secret key to how Venture Capital Works um most kinds of investing uh you think about a normal distribution uh so that's where there's a big bell curve and most of the Investments you might make uh will have a return a gain or a loss something near the average um but with with uh Venture Capital most of them fail because most startups fail but there are a few that make enormous profits 10 times 20 times the investment and that's a very different kind of investing and forces you to think differently and so I thought the power law was the right title yes well with the writing process um the key thing for me is always to speak to the most important people in the field and so I take a long time because these people are busy and important and usually they say no when I first asked them so it takes about four or five years to see all the people I need to see and uh interview maybe 200 or so top

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Power Law Is a Network Discipline
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Transcript-derived: Mallaby's original English transcript makes venture capital look less like normal investing and more like an access game built around extreme outliers. The writing process he describes also implies that durable VC edge is embedded in relationships with the important people before the story is obvious.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Venture capital's core underwriting error is treating startup outcomes like a bell curve; the actual game is building enough network access to own the rare 10x to 20x outcomes.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Prefer VC platforms and emerging managers that can improve access to outlier founders through deep networks, historical pattern recognition, and downturn discipline.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode pairs Mallaby's power-law explanation with a comparison of Silicon Valley and China venture scenes, making the framework relevant during funding downturns and market resets.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Startup investing shifts from average-return portfolio construction to concentrated outlier discovery, where social networks and institutional memory are core assets.
evidenceSignals: Mallaby says the power law is the secret key to how venture capital works
He contrasts venture outcomes with a normal distribution
He says most startups fail but a few return 10 or 20 times the investment
He describes spending four or five years interviewing about 200 top people
The episode includes a section on venture capital in China
watchlist: Fund managers with proprietary founder access rather than only capital
Portfolio construction concentrated enough to benefit from outliers
Downturn behavior by Silicon Valley and China VC firms
Cross-border differences in network density and exit paths
Evidence that managers can repeatedly win competitive allocations
risks: Transcript excerpt is partial despite caption transcript coverage
Power-law awareness is common; execution and access are scarce
Concentration increases fund volatility
China VC exits and regulation may weaken Silicon Valley analogies

thesisTable.implication: Portfolio math must tolerate many failures

thesisTable.evidence: He cites rare 10x and 20x winners
thesisTable.implication: Allocation access to the best startups is the central moat

thesisTable.implication: VC edge is partly accumulated social knowledge

episodes.titleZh: 8. 和畅销书作家马拉比聊《The Power Law》：硅谷风投的起源、秘密和大人物肖像
episodes.titleEn: 8. Talking With Bestselling Author Sebastian Mallaby About The Power Law: The Origins, Secrets, and Major Figures of Silicon Valley VC
episodes.summaryEn: This interview with Sebastian Mallaby explores the origins of Silicon Valley venture capital and the power-law logic that lets a few outsized wins compensate for many failures. The discussion profiles Arthur Rock, Don Valentine, Tom Perkins, Sequoia's succession culture, Masayoshi Son, Yuri Milner, and the contrasts between Silicon Valley and Chinese venture capital.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
我们对中国风投的大人物如数家珍，但对风投在硅谷的起源、进程和隐秘故事鲜少涉猎。
我们可能了解沈南鹏，却不了解红杉资本创办人。
我们可能了解凯鹏华盈在中国止步不前，却不了解这家曾与红杉共享"双子星"美誉的基金，在硅谷是如何下坠的？
最近，我采访了塞巴斯蒂安·马拉比（Sebastian Mallaby），他是一名英国记者和作家。他出版过记录对冲基金历史的《More Money Than God》（富可敌国）、格林斯潘传记《The Man Who Knew》（格林斯潘传），两次入围普利策，而他的新书《The Power Law》（风险投资史）揭露了硅谷风投的内幕故事。
The Power Law可以翻译成幂次法则或指数法则，它揭示风险投资的秘密规律，即大多数项目终将失败，但只要赌中少数几个有巨额回报的大项目，就能赚得盆满钵满。
在两小时的采访中，我和马拉比聊了硅谷风投的起源、秘密，以及那些撼动风投史的大人物们的性格与命运——可以看到，他们可都是一些怪家伙。
（感谢刘一鸣对本期播客的中文配音支持）

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Chinese listeners may know many major figures in China's venture-capital world, but know far less about how venture capital began and developed in Silicon Valley. This interview with Sebastian Mallaby, a British journalist and author, discusses his new book The Power Law and the inside stories it tells. The title refers to venture capital's secret rule: most investments fail, but a few deals with enormous returns can make the whole fund hugely profitable. Over two hours, the conversation covers VC's origins, secrets, and the personalities and fates of the people who shaped its history.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: VC Franchises Need Succession, Not Stars
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The Power Law interview suggests Sequoia's durability is an institutional-design story, not just a story about famous investors. Silence in meetings, succession culture, and founder access are repeatable behaviors that can outlast individuals.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: The highest-quality VC firms should be valued less like star managers and more like professional service franchises with codified succession and access rituals.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Allocate to venture managers that demonstrate institutional succession, disciplined selection behavior, and durable founder networks rather than relying on a single charismatic partner.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode introduces Chinese listeners to Silicon Valley VC origins at a time when China venture needed to reassess fund quality, exits, and institutional durability.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Venture capital is moving from personality-driven capital deployment toward franchise systems that preserve access and judgment across generations.
evidenceSignals: The episode profiles Arthur Rock, Don Valentine, Tom Perkins, and Sequoia leaders
It asks why Sequoia has stayed on top for so long
Don Valentine is noted for not naming the fund after himself and staying silent in meetings
The notes cover three generations of Sequoia successors
Masayoshi Son and Yuri Milner are framed as game changers from outside the system
watchlist: Partner succession at top-tier venture firms
Founder references that mention decision process rather than brand alone
Allocation discipline during hot markets
Outside capital sources changing pricing behavior
China VC firms building enduring institutions rather than founder brands
risks: Notes-derived source is based on a book interview, not fund-performance data
Past franchise strength may not survive platform shifts
Brand can mask weak current partner judgment
Large outside capital can distort power-law discipline

thesisTable.implication: Manager diligence should inspect process continuity

thesisTable.implication: Capital abundance can weaken traditional VC discipline

episodes.titleZh: 7. 和谈婧聊：硅谷冻卵历险记和Web3环球旅行
episodes.titleEn: 7. Talking With Tan Jing: A Silicon Valley Egg-Freezing Adventure and a Web3 World Tour
episodes.summaryEn: Tan Jing describes two kinds of adventure: freezing her eggs in Silicon Valley under documentary cameras and traveling across more than ten global Web3 hubs as an investor. The episode connects bodily autonomy, changed views on intimacy and marriage, and a Web3 movement that she frames as globalization continuing in a different domain.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
这是两场冒险。
第一场是作为女性的冒险。2019年，谈婧赴硅谷，在纪录片镜头下完成冻卵。本集节目，她详细描述了冻卵前的崩溃大哭、冻卵期间的疼与痛苦，意外的是，冻卵半年后迎来惊喜反转。某种意义上，是通过一次"反婚姻"行为，达成了女性主体性，以及对亲密关系和婚姻的接纳。
第二场冒险是在商业上。过去半年，她作为投资人拿着三个大箱子落地了全球十多个地方。这群狂热的Web3信徒，像是发起一场针对逆全球化时代的嬉皮士运动。她说，Globalization never ends - it is happening in another domain。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: This is a story of two adventures. The first is a woman's adventure: in 2019 Tan Jing went to Silicon Valley and froze her eggs while being filmed for a documentary. She describes crying before the procedure, the pain during it, and an unexpected reversal half a year later; in a sense, an act against marriage helped her reach female subjectivity and accept intimacy and marriage. The second adventure is commercial. Over the past half year, she traveled with three large suitcases to more than ten places worldwide, following Web3 believers who seemed to be launching a movement against deglobalization.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Autonomy Is the Common Asset
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Tan Jing's egg-freezing and Web3 stories look unrelated, but both are about optionality under institutional pressure. Bodily autonomy and borderless financial communities become parallel forms of control over time, identity, and belonging.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Web3's durable value may be less token speculation and more infrastructure for mobile communities that continue globalization when institutions fragment.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Look for products that expand individual optionality: fertility services with strong ethics and care quality, and global community or identity infrastructure for mobile builders.
alphaLens.whyNow: The source frames Web3 believers as launching a movement against deglobalization while Tan's fertility story shows changing expectations around marriage and subjectivity.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Personal and commercial globalization are moving from inherited institutions toward opt-in networks, self-directed timing, and portable identity.
evidenceSignals: Tan Jing froze her eggs in Silicon Valley in 2019 under documentary cameras
She describes a reversal toward female subjectivity after egg freezing
She traveled through more than ten global Web3 hubs as an investor
The source frames Web3 believers as a movement against deglobalization
The episode connects intimacy, marriage, autonomy, and venture investing
watchlist: Fertility clinic demand and patient-outcome transparency
Regulation and consumer trust in egg-freezing services
Web3 communities that retain builders through downturns
Identity, wallet, and governance tools for global contributors
Female-founder and autonomy-focused consumer health markets
risks: Notes-derived source is personal and may not generalize
Fertility services carry medical, ethical, and emotional risk
Web3 narratives can collapse into token speculation
Deglobalization can restrict the mobility Web3 communities depend on

episodes.titleZh: 6. 显微镜下的硅谷大裁员：一笔笔现实经济账
episodes.titleEn: 6. Silicon Valley Layoffs Under the Microscope: The Practical Economics Line by Line
episodes.summaryEn: Meta product manager Neal Zhang recounts the week of Meta's largest layoffs from the perspective of someone who had also recently experienced China's education-sector crackdown. Recorded on November 11, 2022, after the cuts settled, the episode covers the emotional toll, severance math, immigration status, tech compensation, living costs, and career advice.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
Neal Zhang是Meta产品经理，在去年刚经历完国内双减，今年人在硅谷，又亲历了Meta史上最大规模的裁员风波。可谓在双重打击下绝地求生。
这期播客录制时间是美国西部时间的2022年11月11日，也就是在Meta裁员尘埃落定的周五晚上，他以亲历者的视角讲述了这"黑色系一周"到底发生了什么，以及自己的心路历程。
我们还聊到华人在硅谷的真实生活状态、收支状况、中美薪酬对比和离职补偿金的现实话题。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Neal Zhang is a product manager at Meta. After going through China's double-reduction shock last year, he was in Silicon Valley this year and personally experienced the largest layoff wave in Meta's history, surviving a double blow. This episode was recorded on Friday night, November 11, 2022, US Pacific time, after the Meta layoffs had settled. From a participant's perspective, he explains what happened during that dark week and what he went through emotionally, while also discussing the real lives of Chinese people in Silicon Valley, income and expenses, China-US pay comparisons, and severance.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Big-Tech Jobs Become Risk Portfolios
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Neal Zhang's Meta layoff account turns compensation into only one line item in a larger risk ledger. Immigration status, severance math, living costs, and frozen hiring make big-tech employment look like a leveraged position rather than a guaranteed safe asset.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Layoffs can increase startup opportunity not simply by releasing talent, but by forcing high-skill workers to price visa risk, liquidity, and career convexity.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back career, immigration, compensation-planning, and founder-transition tools for high-skilled tech workers whose employment risk is now explicit.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode was recorded just after Meta's November 2022 layoffs settled, when Silicon Valley workers were recalculating severance, immigration windows, and job-market freeze risk.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: The Silicon Valley professional wage worker is moving from perceived security to active risk management across employer, visa, and cash-flow exposure.

episodes.titleZh: 5. 和黄宇翔聊东南亚6国：政治、杀戮和国父
episodes.titleEn: 5. Talking With Huang Yuxiang About Six Southeast Asian Countries: Politics, Killing, and Founding Fathers
episodes.summaryEn: This Southeast Asia political guide looks at Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, Vietnam, and Singapore through political integration, founding fathers, and violent turning points. The episode argues that economic potential often sits on top of hardened political orders created through conflict.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
【到全世界创业生活】第2期
东南亚是我们最陌生的近邻。很多人落地第一反应是，哦，这里竟然有11个国家，历史、文化、政治、宗教渊源大有不同。
本期节目，我邀请了《亚洲周刊》策划编辑黄宇翔。出于兴趣和工作，他一直站在中国香港遥望东南亚。他经常被同事说成「屠夫型记者」，因为他格外关注一个民族的暴力事件及其呈现的民族性。我们这期以政治为切口，揭开东南亚各国政治演变历程——它也是一部冲突史。
我们重点聊了东南亚6国：印度尼西亚——马来西亚——菲律宾——泰国——越南——新加坡。
可以将这期节目当作东南亚政治导览。你会近乎残忍地发现，今天经济发展最具潜力的国家，是站在由血腥杀戮带来的稳固政权之上。经济的脚下是政治，政治的脚下是血骨。
【主持】
张小珺，商业作者（微博@张小珺-Benita (https://weibo.com/u/6486678714) ）
【嘉宾】
黄宇翔，《亚洲周刊》策划编辑
【你会听到】

17:52第一站：印尼

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Southeast Asia is our most unfamiliar neighbor. Many people arrive and are surprised to learn that it has 11 countries with very different histories, cultures, politics, and religious roots. In this episode, Asian Weekly planning editor Huang Yuxiang joins to examine the political evolution of Southeast Asian countries through the lens of politics and conflict. The focus is on Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, Vietnam, and Singapore. It can be heard as a political guide to Southeast Asia, one that almost brutally reveals how today's most promising economies may stand on stable regimes built through bloody violence.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: ASEAN Alpha Starts With Political Memory
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The episode argues that Southeast Asian growth stories sit on top of political orders forged through violence, integration, and founding compromises. That makes political memory a commercial diligence input rather than a history lesson.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: ASEAN's investability cannot be inferred from demographics or GDP growth alone; country-specific state formation determines how business actually compounds.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Invest behind local political-risk intelligence, partner selection, and country-specific market entry across Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, Vietnam, and Singapore.
alphaLens.whyNow: The source says Chinese research on Southeast Asia is thin just as entrepreneurs increasingly see the region as their unfamiliar neighbor and growth frontier.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Southeast Asia is moving from a generic China-plus-one slogan to a set of distinct political economies that require historically informed operating models.

episodes.titleZh: 4.【到全世界创业生活】寻访越南：最后一个待开掘的儒家社会
episodes.titleEn: 4. Startup Life Around the World: Visiting Vietnam, the Last Untapped Confucian Society
episodes.summaryEn: Francis Yang explains why he moved to Vietnam, why his happiness rose sharply there, and why he sees the country as a final Confucian-influenced emerging market. The episode mixes serious questions about supply-chain spillover, development prospects, IT outsourcing, labor, housing, and business risks with observations of Hanoi, Ho Chi Minh City, food, safety, and everyday life.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
本期嘉宾杨帆（Francis）是上海人，墨尔本念书，在香港、上海做律师和投资；但最终，1年前他决定安家越南。据他说，搬到越南以后，幸福指数直线拉高，他未来不仅会一直生活在这，还要娶越南太太，孩子上学也要在越南。我表示难以置信。而这种诧异代表了很多人对越南的信息滞后。
让我们带上耳朵去旅行。在这期节目，我和Francis一起，打开了各种越南严肃话题（产业链外溢、越南会成为世界工厂、发达国家吗？），同时勾勒了这个国家经济和社会生活的真实面貌。可以跟随他的脚步，漫步河内的秋、胡志明的现代街头，看下了班打渔回家的Grab司机，感受这个浪漫国度里的暧昧和女性味道。
播客最后，你能听出弥漫在西贡的粉红泡泡。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Guest Francis Yang is from Shanghai, studied in Melbourne, and worked in law and investment in Hong Kong and Shanghai before deciding a year ago to settle in Vietnam. He says his happiness rose sharply after the move, and that he plans to keep living there, marry a Vietnamese wife, and have his children study there, which the host found hard to believe. That surprise reflects how outdated many people's information about Vietnam is. This episode opens up serious topics such as supply-chain spillover and whether Vietnam can become a world factory or a developed country, while also portraying its economic and social life.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Vietnam Is Founder Habitat, Not Just Factory Floor
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Francis Yang's Vietnam move reframes the country as a place to live and build, not just a supply-chain spillover destination. The Confucian familiarity, housing experience, happiness delta, and manufacturing discussion together point to a founder-habitat thesis.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Vietnam's opportunity is not simply cheaper labor; it is a culturally legible emerging market that can attract operators, families, and long-duration company builders.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back Vietnam-linked manufacturing services, IT outsourcing, housing, local consumer infrastructure, and founder-support networks that benefit from both supply-chain movement and lifestyle pull.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode says Vietnam became popular in China over the prior two years and asks whether supply-chain spillover can make it a world factory or developed economy.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Vietnam is moving in Chinese founder perception from outdated stereotype to a plausible long-term base for work, life, and company formation.

The notes discuss supply-chain spillover and world-factory potential

The episode covers IT outsourcing, labor, housing, safety, Hanoi, and Ho Chi Minh City

episodes.titleZh: 3. 宇航员与机器人：漫谈深邃浪漫的ROBOT新知
episodes.titleEn: 3. Astronauts and Robots: A Deep and Romantic Conversation About Robot Knowledge
episodes.summaryEn: A robotics founder and unicorn CEO discusses robots more broadly after the prior episode's investor-focused look at humanoids. The conversation compares humanoid robots to moon landings, questions whether human form will dominate, places robotics at an early internet-like stage, and reflects on startup risk, industrial robots, aerospace funding, and founder models.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
上期播客我们从马斯克发布人形机器人擎天柱聊起，从投资人视角，聊到了宏观的机器人产业格局。这期我们也聊机器人，不过更轻松，嘉宾是一名6年的机器人创业者，也是一家独角兽公司CEO。他从清华和慕尼黑工大毕业以后，先在德国公司做机器人，16年转战国内开始创业。
和他聊天，更像是听了一场robot漫谈。

•  03:09 马斯克的"讲故事"能力有多惊人
•  07:40 人形不一定会是robot主流形态
•  09:14 大众期待和人类真实技术中间差了50年

•  21:54 "机器"和"机器人"的区别

•  25:23 电影《环太平洋》里的spirit
•  29:14 机器人如今阶段类似80年代的互联网
•  39:18 机器人创业途中凶险时分：一击不中，就跪了
•  43:00 1个亿意味什么
•  46:38 航空航天曾是机器人行业的金主
•  49:45 雷军是可学企业家的天花板，马斯克、乔布斯天赋异禀、谁学谁死
•  51:43 工业机器人的两步走：从车厂到其他

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: In the previous episode we started from Musk's release of the humanoid robot Optimus and discussed the broad robotics industry from an investor's perspective. This episode also discusses robots, but in a more relaxed way. The guest is a robotics entrepreneur of six years and CEO of a unicorn company. After graduating from Tsinghua and the Technical University of Munich, he first worked on robots at a German company, then returned to China to start a company in 2016. Talking with him feels more like listening to a free-ranging robot conversation.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Robotics Is 1980s Internet With Hardware Consequences
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: The robotics founder's analogy to the 1980s internet is a warning against both dismissal and overprecision. Humanoid moon-shot narratives may attract attention, but the real investable field remains uncertain form factors, industrial niches, and founders who survive costly mistakes.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: Humanoid robots may be the wrong default form; the broader robotics stack can compound before humanoids become mainstream.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Back task-specific robotics, industrial automation, components, control systems, and safety layers where value is visible before general humanoid capability arrives.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode follows Musk's Optimus reveal, using public excitement to discuss the gap between expectations and actual robotics capability.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Robotics is moving from machine automation toward embodied systems, but the dominant forms and business models remain as unsettled as the early internet.
evidenceSignals: The guest compares humanoid robots to a moon landing
He says humanoid form may not become the mainstream robot form
Public expectations and real technology are described as 50 years apart
Robotics is compared to the internet in the 1980s
The notes warn that missing once in robotics entrepreneurship can take a company down
watchlist: Industrial robot deployments with clear ROI
Component cost declines in actuators, sensors, and control systems
Humanoid pilots that move beyond staged demos
Founder burn rate and hardware iteration cycles
Aerospace and industrial funding spillovers into robotics
risks: Notes-derived source is broad and intentionally exploratory
Hardware mistakes can be fatal and expensive
Humanoid hype can misallocate capital
Customer adoption may lag technical progress

thesisTable.evidence: The guest says it may not become mainstream
thesisTable.implication: Invest across task-specific robotics, not only humanoids

thesisTable.claim: The field is early but consequential
thesisTable.evidence: Robotics is compared to the internet in the 1980s
thesisTable.implication: Long-duration capital can benefit from infrastructure formation

thesisTable.evidence: The notes say one miss can take a robotics startup down
thesisTable.implication: Milestone financing and customer validation are essential

episodes.titleZh: 2. 和曹巍聊聊马斯克、机器人恐怖谷和《西部世界》
episodes.titleEn: 2. Talking With Cao Wei About Musk, the Robotics Uncanny Valley, and Westworld
episodes.summaryEn: Investor Cao Wei analyzes Tesla's humanoid robot, the technologies that can and cannot transfer from autonomous driving, and the scenarios where a human form actually matters. The episode also covers the uncanny valley, Boston Dynamics, service robots, negative externalities, and why a robotics wave may offer Chinese companies a rare systemic opportunity.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
前不久，马斯克发布叫擎天柱的人形机器人，我采访了一系列中国机器人公司CEO和投资人。想要听听他们对这款备受期待机器人的想法，也想看看正在爆发的中国机器人产业发展到什么阶段了。
本集嘉宾是投资人曹巍，他从5-6年前就专注研究机器人。聊天话题很有意思也非常硬核，包括马斯克的机器人和他的终极梦想、人形机器人进入家庭可能面临的恐怖谷效应，以及机器人产业宏观演变和未来格局。他觉得，机器人浪潮对中国企业来说是无与伦比的新机会。

•  04:31 一款人形机器人需要满足哪些要素

•  19:30 "恐怖谷效应"
•  25:40 马斯克又做车，又做航天，又做人形机器人，这几个方向对人类未来意味什么？
•  25:34 没准马斯克是《西部世界》粉丝，100年后还活着

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: Not long ago, Musk released a humanoid robot called Optimus, and the host interviewed a series of Chinese robotics CEOs and investors to hear their views on this highly anticipated robot and to see where China's fast-growing robotics industry stands. This episode's guest is investor Cao Wei, who has focused on robotics for five or six years. The conversation is both interesting and technical, covering Musk's robot and ultimate dream, the uncanny valley that home humanoid robots may face, the macro evolution of the robotics industry, its future structure, and the new opportunity for Chinese companies.

alphaLens.alphaTitle: Optimus Does Not Make Every Robot Humanoid
alphaLens.hiddenInsight: Notes-derived: Cao Wei's analysis makes Tesla's Optimus a forcing function for robotics diligence rather than proof that humanoids will dominate. The key is which autonomy capabilities transfer from cars, which do not, and where human form is economically necessary.
alphaLens.nonConsensusView: The robotics wave can create a rare systemic opportunity for Chinese companies without humanoids overturning every service and industrial scenario.
alphaLens.investmentThesis: Invest in robotics components, fine-control systems, manufacturing supply chains, and scenario-specific robots where human form solves a real environmental constraint.
alphaLens.whyNow: The episode is tied to Tesla AI Day and Musk's Optimus reveal, when public attention moved faster than fine-grained control capability.
alphaLens.fundamentalShift: Robotics is shifting from isolated demos toward scenario economics, where embodiment, control, and manufacturing depth determine investability.
evidenceSignals: The episode asks what can carry over from autonomous driving to Tesla robots and what cannot
Fine-grained control difficulty is a specific topic
Cao Wei says it is wrong to claim humanoid robots can overturn everything
The notes ask in what scenarios robots need human form
The episode covers the uncanny valley and Chinese robotics opportunity
watchlist: Tesla Optimus milestones in manipulation and autonomy
Chinese robotics startups with supply-chain or control advantages
Service scenarios where human form reduces environment redesign
Boston Dynamics-style mobility progress versus commercial deployment
Consumer acceptance and uncanny-valley reactions
risks: Notes-derived source predates later humanoid demonstrations
Tesla's narrative can inflate private robotics valuations
Fine motor control may remain too hard for broad deployment
Human-like robots can create safety and acceptance issues

thesisTable.evidence: The episode asks what carries over from autonomous driving and what cannot
thesisTable.implication: Robotics diligence must separate perception from manipulation

thesisTable.evidence: The notes ask where robots actually need human form
thesisTable.implication: Avoid humanoid theses without environment-specific ROI

thesisTable.claim: China may have a systemic opportunity
thesisTable.evidence: The source frames robotics as a rare opportunity for Chinese companies
thesisTable.implication: Manufacturing depth and components can be strategic advantages

episodes.titleZh: 1. 和投资人梦秋聊聊加州、投资寒潮和林黛玉
episodes.titleEn: 1. Talking With Investor Mengqiu About California, the Investment Chill, and Lin Daiyu
episodes.summaryEn: Wang Mengqiu of Crystal Stream Capital reflects on investment cycles, the current capital winter, career choices, and the deeper macro changes shaping venture investing. The conversation also turns playful, asking which Dream of the Red Chamber characters would make entrepreneurs or top Bilibili creators.
episodes.sourceZhExcerpt:
在《仅仅是昨天：我们亲历的萧条、繁荣和时代》里，清流资本创始合伙人王梦秋女士，是我采访的一位嘉宾。
但我觉得，我们俩聊天的好玩程度要远远高于文中节选片段。
在征求了她的同意后，我把两个多小时的聊天做成了这期播客。
我们不仅仅聊了最近的投资环境，她所经历过的资本周期以及周期对她职业生涯的影响，还聊了一些有意思的话题——比方说，大观园里谁最有可能成为创业者、企业家；要是把林黛玉、薛宝钗放在今天，谁更有可能成为千万顶流的B站UP主？我收获了许多意料之外的答案。

episodes.sourceEnExcerpt: In Just Yesterday: The Depressions, Booms, and Times We Experienced, Crystal Stream Capital founding partner Wang Mengqiu was one of the interviewees. But the host felt their conversation was far more interesting than the excerpts in the article, and, with Wang's permission, turned more than two hours of discussion into this episode. They talk not only about the recent investment environment, the capital cycles Wang has experienced, and how those cycles affected her career, but also lighter questions, such as who in Grand View Garden would be most likely to become an entrepreneur, and whether Lin Daiyu or Xue Baochai could become a top Bilibili creator.
